original_question.question: Why does the Transactional Model describe communication as a co-constructed, simultaneous process rather than a simple one-way encoding/decoding sequence?

options.A: Because simultaneity guarantees a fixed, context-independent meaning.
options.B: Because ongoing, simultaneous encoding/decoding by all participants allows meanings to be shaped by each other's frames of reference, so understanding emerges from interaction.
options.C: Because the model assumes a perfectly noiseless channel that preserves the original encoding without distortion.
options.D: Because it treats feedback as unnecessary, letting the sender control the interpretation entirely.

concepts_tested: The Linear, Interactional, and Transactional models of communication and how their directionality, encoding/decoding, and simultaneity differ.
The two overarching perspectives on communication: transmission (information exchange) and ritual (work to connect and enable that exchange), and what each implies about purpose and process.
The role of social context and norms in shaping language use and communication behavior (why formality varies with context, and how sociolinguistic factors influence communication).
parent_concepts: Interpersonal Deception Theory (IDT): examines how communicative context and sender/receiver cognitions and behaviors interact to produce deceptive exchanges.
Encoding/Decoding model: producers encode meanings into texts; audiences decode them (with possible negotiation or opposition), leading to varying interpretations.

Cultural/social context shapes interpretation: shared backgrounds lead to similar readings, while divergent backgrounds can produce aberrant decoding (interpretations that differ from the intended meaning).
Concept 1: Media constructs reality by prioritizing certain issues and assigning salience, thereby shaping what the public perceives as important.
Concept 1: Multiplexing and transmission channels enable simultaneous communication over a single medium (why/how channels are used to increase capacity).
Relationship between digital processing advantages (e.g., error detection/correction, data compression) and the use of different domains (time, frequency, space) and linear vs. nonlinear operations
Concept 1: Sign models and interpretation (Saussure's dyadic vs. Peirce's triadic) and how these structures produce meaning.
Concept 1: Modulation as a mechanism for encoding audio onto radio waves (AM vs FM) and the trade-offs between amplitude and frequency modulation.
Concept 2: The relationship and distinctions between risk communication and crisis communication (scope, goals, and outcomes) and why this distinction matters.
Balancing the message, the media channel, and the audience as a core mechanism to achieve long-term organizational goals.
Concept 3: Evaluation principle: effectiveness is measured by audience comprehension (not solely aesthetic judgment).
Concept 1: Media function as environments that influence human perception, understanding, feeling, value, and even survival.

pass_1_attempt.question: Why does the Transactional Model describe communication as co-constructed and simultaneous rather than a simple one-way encoding/decoding sequence?

options.A: Because simultaneity guarantees a fixed, context-independent meaning.
options.B: Because participants encode and decode at the same time, and each person's frame of reference shapes meaning so understanding emerges through interaction.
options.C: Because it assumes a perfectly noiseless channel that preserves the sender's original message.
options.D: Because it treats feedback as unnecessary, letting the sender fully control interpretation.

pass_1_attempt.simplification_notes: Shortened the question to one clear sentence, kept technical terms (encode/decode, frame of reference) with simple wording; options made concise and plausible while preserving the original answer.

pass_2_attempt.question: At a school: (1) the principal reads a prepared announcement to the whole student body, (2) a teacher and student exchange questions and answers during class, (3) a group video meeting has overlapping speech, lag, and members with different backgrounds. Which option best matches each event with the correct communication model, whether it reflects the transmission or ritual perspective, and why formality changes with context?

options.A: (1) Linear — transmission; high formality because institutional role and norms. (2) Interactional — ritual; back-and-forth feedback and connection, so moderate formality per classroom norms. (3) Transactional — transmission; simultaneous send/receive through a noisy channel, formality varies as people adjust to social frames and tech constraints.
options.B: (1) Transactional — ritual; principal and students exchange messages simultaneously, so low formality. (2) Linear — transmission; teacher simply sends info with no feedback, so very formal. (3) Interactional — ritual; video meeting is cooperative with little noise, so uniform formality.
options.C: (1) Interactional — ritual; the assembly is cooperative and mutual, so informal. (2) Transactional — transmission; teacher and student speak at once with no feedback, so formality is fixed by role. (3) Linear — transmission; video meeting is one-way broadcast, so high formality.
options.D: (1) Linear — ritual; the principal connects people more than transmits data, so formality is low. (2) Interactional — transmission; classroom talk is only about exchanging data with no relation effects. (3) Transactional — ritual; video meeting is mainly about bonding, so formality does not change.

pass_2_attempt.generation_notes: Created a three-part school scenario and matched each part to Linear/Interactional/Transactional models, paired with transmission or ritual perspective, and tied formality differences to social norms and context.
concepts_tested: Linear vs Interactional vs Transactional models (directionality, feedback, simultaneity)
Transmission vs ritual perspectives and implications for purpose/process
Role of social context and norms in shaping language formality

original_question.question: Which statement best captures how stage-based psychosocial theories and dynamic systems approaches differ in explaining how a major identity-related transition occurs during adolescence, and what this implies for predicting individual paths?

options.A: Stage-based theories propose a series of fixed, age-linked crises that produce discrete shifts in identity, producing relatively predictable trajectories; dynamic systems view identity as the outcome of continuous, context-sensitive interactions among biology, cognition, and environment, yielding nonlinear, highly variable paths.
options.B: Stage-based theories propose identity changes are continuous and uniform across individuals; dynamic systems propose abrupt changes at fixed ages.
options.C: Stage-based theories claim environment alone shapes identity; dynamic systems claim genes alone shape identity.
options.D: Stage-based theories assume no transition occurs; dynamic systems assume identity never changes.

concepts_tested: Concept 1: Nature vs. nurture and biopsychosocial interactions as the engine of development (how genes, environment, and personal characteristics interact to shape changes over time)
Concept 2: Interdependence of the three developmental dimensions (physical, cognitive, social-emotional) and how changes in one dimension influence others across the lifespan
Concept 3: Theoretical frameworks for development (stage-based psychosocial theories like Erikson vs. dynamic systems/alternative models) and how they account for change and transitions
parent_concepts: Secure attachment results from sensitive, responsive, and consistently available caregiving, and serves as a secure base for a child to explore the world.

The Strange Situation Procedure provides a framework for classifying attachment patterns (secure, avoidant, anxious, disorganized) but findings may reflect social/environmental contexts rather than fixed traits, highlighting measurement and universality considerations.

Concept 1: Ecological systems framework applied to PYD — multiple environments influence youth development and there are reciprocal, mutually beneficial relations between youth and their social contexts.
Cues and mechanisms used to infer friendship in childhood (sharing resources, sharing secrets, propinquity, loyalty)
Concept 1: Psychological and moral maturation as the mechanism of development — growth results from experiences and challenges that reshape the protagonist from childhood to adulthood.

pass_1_attempt.question: Which statement best explains how stage-based psychosocial theories (like Erikson's) and dynamic systems approaches differ in describing an identity change during adolescence, and what this means for predicting individual paths?

options.A: Stage-based theories say fixed, age-linked crises cause discrete identity shifts and give relatively predictable trajectories; dynamic systems say identity emerges from continuous, context-sensitive interactions of biology, cognition, and environment, producing nonlinear, highly variable paths.
options.B: Stage-based theories say identity changes are continuous and similar for everyone; dynamic systems say identity changes are sudden at fixed ages.
options.C: Stage-based theories say the environment alone shapes identity; dynamic systems say genes alone determine identity.
options.D: Stage-based theories claim no identity transition happens in adolescence; dynamic systems claim identity never changes.

pass_1_attempt.simplification_notes: Shortened wording, named Erikson as an example, removed dense jargon, and kept the contrast between fixed stage crises versus continuous, interaction-based change; options made concise and plausible.

pass_2_attempt.question: Maya hits puberty earlier than her peers, her parents divorce, and she begins both to think more abstractly and to experience mood swings; which explanation best integrates genes, environment, and interacting physical, cognitive and social–emotional changes while rejecting only fixed stage logic?

options.A: Genetic determinism: Maya's early puberty and mood changes are caused entirely by her genes, with little influence from the environment.
options.B: Erikson's stage model: these events show an adolescent 'Identity vs. Role Confusion' crisis that occurs as a discrete stage shaping her personality.
options.C: A biopsychosocial dynamic-systems account: genes influence puberty timing, family stress alters emotion regulation and neural plasticity, and physical, cognitive and social–emotional changes continuously interact across contexts.
options.D: Piagetian stage change: Maya's improved abstract thinking shows entry into formal operations, and her emotions and social role adjust afterward as secondary effects.

pass_2_attempt.generation_notes: Created a brief scenario (puberty + divorce + cognitive/emotional change) and offered four concise theoretical explanations; correct answer links biopsychosocial interactions, cross-dimension influence, and dynamic-systems perspective vs. strict stage views.
concepts_tested: Nature vs. nurture and biopsychosocial interactions
Interdependence of physical, cognitive, and social–emotional development
Stage-based theories (Erikson/Piaget) versus dynamic-systems/biopsychosocial models

original_question.question: How does parental history and psychopathology influence child development primarily through parental sensitivity?

options.A: It changes the child's genetic predispositions so that parental responsiveness no longer affects outcomes.
options.B: It can reduce the parent's emotional availability and timely responsiveness to the child's cues, lowering parental sensitivity, which in turn shapes the child's attachment quality and subsequent development.
options.C: It strengthens parental control to the point that sensitivity becomes irrelevant.
options.D: It determines child outcomes entirely through environmental factors outside parental care, making sensitivity a non-mediator.

concepts_tested: Concept 1: The four parenting styles (authoritative, authoritarian, permissive, uninvolved) are defined by combinations of acceptance/responsiveness and demand/control, and these styles relate to different child mental health and well-being outcomes.
Concept 2: Parental history and psychopathology influence parental sensitivity, which mediates child outcomes (attachment quality and subsequent development).

Concept 3: Warm adoptive parenting is associated with reductions in internalizing and externalizing problems over time for adopted children, illustrating how parenting climate affects long-term child outcomes.
parent_concepts: Secure attachment results from sensitive, responsive, and consistently available caregiving, and serves as a secure base for a child to explore the world.

pass_1_attempt.question: How can a parent's past experiences and mental health problems affect a child's development through parental sensitivity?

options.A: They change the child's genes so parental responsiveness no longer matters.
options.B: They can reduce the parent's emotional availability and timely response to the child's cues, lowering parental sensitivity and shaping the child's attachment and later development.
options.C: They make parents so controlling that parental sensitivity becomes irrelevant.
options.D: They influence child outcomes only through other environmental factors, so parental sensitivity does not mediate development.

pass_1_attempt.simplification_notes: Question shortened to one sentence and 'parental sensitivity' clarified as emotional availability/responsiveness; answer choices made concise and kept the same correct option.

pass_2_attempt.question: Four families raise an adopted 6-year-old: Family A sets clear rules and explains them while showing warmth; Family B enforces strict obedience with little emotional support; Family C allows freedom with few rules; Family D is emotionally distant and minimally involved. Which option best predicts the child's long-term mental health given that parental history can reduce parental sensitivity and that warm adoptive parenting lowers internalizing and externalizing problems?

options.A: Family A is authoritative (high responsiveness, moderate control) and most likely to support good mental health; if parents have unresolved trauma that lowers sensitivity it can harm attachment unless treated, but warm adoptive parenting tends to reduce internalizing and externalizing problems over time.
options.B: Family B's strict, low-responsiveness (authoritarian) approach is best because rigid rules prevent problems; parental history rarely affects parental sensitivity, and warm parenting has little impact on long-term outcomes.
options.C: Family C (permissive) produces the best results because high acceptance and low demands let children learn freely; parental psychopathology mainly affects biological children and not adopted ones.
options.D: Family D (uninvolved) will build the child's independence and resilience; parental history only changes material resources, and rewards or gifts are the main factor that reduces internalizing and externalizing problems.

pass_2_attempt.generation_notes: Created a scenario mapping four families to Baumrind's four styles, then asked students to apply links between style (responsiveness/control), parental history affecting sensitivity/attachment, and evidence that warm adoptive parenting reduces internalizing/externalizing problems.
concepts_tested: Parenting styles defined by acceptance/responsiveness and demand/control
Parental history and psychopathology influencing parental sensitivity and child attachment/outcomes
Warm adoptive parenting reducing internalizing and externalizing problems over time

original_question.question: Why does language development typically depend on both maturation (genetic readiness) and environmental input, rather than on either factor alone?

options.A: Because maturation creates a neural readiness window (sensitive periods and plasticity) that must be paired with rich linguistic experiences to sculpt the language networks; without environment, potential remains underused, and with environment but no matured neural framework, gains are limited.
options.B: Because language is entirely determined by the environment; genes do not impose any constraints.
options.C: Because genetic factors alone fully determine language development irrespective of input.
options.D: Because environmental input alters genes directly to create language ability.

concepts_tested: Concept 1: Interaction between maturation (genetic factors) and environmental factors in driving development (nature–nurture interplay).
Concept 2: The impact of caregiver/parental relationships and the stability of the family environment on emotional, social, and behavioral development.
Concept 3: The role of developmental theories and models in describing sequences or stages of development and guiding educational practices and policies.
parent_concepts: Secure attachment results from sensitive, responsive, and consistently available caregiving, and serves as a secure base for a child to explore the world.

Concept 3: Participation in youth sports as a protective factor that influences education and behavior (e.g., reduced school dropout, higher college attendance, and reduced risk behaviors like drug use or crime).

pass_1_attempt.question: Why does language development usually need both genetic maturation (brain readiness) and environmental input (talk and interaction), instead of depending on only one of these factors?

options.A: Because maturation creates neural readiness (sensitive periods and plasticity) that must be paired with rich linguistic experience to shape language networks; without input the potential is underused, and without maturation the child can't fully benefit.
options.B: Because language is entirely caused by the environment and genes play no role.
options.C: Because genes alone fully determine language development, so experience does not matter.
options.D: Because environmental input directly changes genes to create language ability.

pass_1_attempt.simplification_notes: Shortened the question to one sentence, used clear terms (brain readiness, sensitive periods, plasticity), and made answer choices concise while keeping the original correct choice and key idea of nature–nurture interaction.

pass_2_attempt.question: A 6-year-old displays advanced vocabulary but has trouble controlling emotions; according to current child development research, which school response best fits the ideas of gene–environment interaction, the importance of stable caregiver relationships, and the use of developmental stage models to guide practice?

options.A: Provide only advanced academic work because cognitive maturation alone determines development.
options.B: Use stage-informed, age-appropriate activities and coordinate with the parents to strengthen stable caregiving while providing emotional support, acknowledging that genes and experience interact.
options.C: Delay any intervention and recommend genetic testing because environment has little effect on emotional problems.
options.D: Move the child to full-time group daycare without family contact to speed socialization.

pass_2_attempt.generation_notes: Created a concrete school scenario asking which intervention aligns with (1) nature–nurture interaction, (2) caregiver stability's role in emotional development, and (3) use of stage-based theories to shape educational practice.
concepts_tested: nature–nurture interaction (maturation and environment)
role of caregiver/parent stability in emotional and social development
use of developmental stage theories/models to guide education and policy

original_question.question: How does including assessments of economic and social conditions in the systematic land-use planning process change the way options are selected, compared to relying solely on physical land potential?

options.A: It focuses decisions on maximizing physical space usage while ignoring markets and community needs.
options.B: It reveals trade-offs between efficiency, equity, and feasibility, ensuring options align with economic realities and social goals before informing the comprehensive plan.
options.C: It primarily slows down decision-making without altering which option is chosen.
options.D: It guarantees that the cheapest available option will always be selected.

concepts_tested: Concept 1: Regulation and zoning as mechanisms to influence land-use behavior to achieve environmental, social, and efficiency goals.
Concept 2: Systematic planning process (assessing land/water potential, alternatives, economic/social conditions) to select the best land-use options and inform a comprehensive plan.
Concept 3: Context-dependency of planning outcomes—the idea that the benefits of land-use regulation depend on location and the specific regulations in place.
parent_concepts: Zoning regulations and their impact on what qualifies as commercial property and how it’s used

pass_1_attempt.question: How does adding an assessment of economic and social conditions to the systematic land-use planning process change how planners select and compare land-use options versus using only physical land potential?

options.A: Decisions focus on maximizing physical space use while ignoring markets and community needs.
options.B: It shows trade-offs between efficiency, equity, and feasibility, so chosen options fit economic realities and social goals before shaping the comprehensive plan.
options.C: It mainly slows decision-making but does not change which option is ultimately chosen.
options.D: It ensures the cheapest option available will always be selected.

pass_1_attempt.simplification_notes: Shortened wording to one clear sentence; replaced technical phrase with 'assessment of economic and social conditions'; kept the comparison to using only physical land potential; shortened answer choices to be concise and plausible.

pass_2_attempt.question: A coastal town with a river that floods sometimes must choose how to allow new housing nearby; which land-use planning action best uses zoning and a systematic assessment to change developer and resident behavior while recognising that outcomes depend on local conditions?

options.A: Adopt zoning that bans housing in identified floodplain after assessing land/water potential, economic and social needs; this directs behavior, reduces conflict and risk, but its benefits depend on enforcement and local site conditions.
options.B: Let developers build where they want but charge a higher permit fee in risky areas; this changes incentives but skips a full land and social assessment and may not prevent harm in all locations.
options.C: Copy a zoning map from a successful inland city without local studies; it applies regulation but ignores river, soil, and community differences so benefits are uncertain.
options.D: Rely only on public education campaigns about flooding and leave land-use rules unchanged; this may inform behavior but lacks regulatory force and a comprehensive plan.

pass_2_attempt.generation_notes: Created a short scenario asking which option integrates zoning, systematic assessment, and context-sensitivity; provided four plausible policy choices with one clearly matching all three core concepts.
concepts_tested: Zoning as a tool to influence land-use behavior
Systematic assessment (land/water, economic/social) informing plans
Context-dependency of planning outcomes (location and regulation matter)

original_question.question: Why does designing with semantic structure and explicit labeling (e.g., meaningful headings, labeled form fields, accessible controls) matter for both direct access and indirect access via assistive technology?

options.A: It makes the interface consistent visually across devices, with no effect on assistive tech.
options.B: It allows assistive technologies to reinterpret content but introduces complexity that can hinder unassisted use.
options.C: It creates a shared, well-structured content model that enables unassisted users to navigate the interface and enables assistive technologies to expose equivalent information and controls to users who rely on them.
options.D: It only benefits users with disabilities and is unnecessary for most users.

concepts_tested: Direct vs. indirect access and the role of assistive technology in accessibility (how design enables unassisted use and compatibility with assistive tech)
The relationship and distinction between accessibility and usability (why they are not the same and how they complement each other)
Universal design vs. accessible design (how these approaches differ philosophically and in practice, and how they aim to maximize usable access for broad user groups)
parent_concepts: A defined walkable catchment (roughly 400–800 meters) around a central transit stop solves the last-mile problem and sets the effective scale of TOD.

pass_1_attempt.question: Why do semantic structure and clear labels (like meaningful headings and labeled form fields) matter for both unassisted users and users who use assistive technology?

options.A: They make the interface look consistent across devices but do not help assistive technologies.
options.B: They let assistive technologies reinterpret content but add complexity that can make unassisted use harder.
options.C: They create a shared, clear content structure so unassisted users can navigate and assistive technologies can expose the same information and controls.
options.D: They only help people with disabilities and are not needed by most users.

pass_1_attempt.simplification_notes: Shortened the question to one clear sentence, replaced jargon with brief examples (headings, labeled fields), and made each option concise while keeping the original choices' meanings.

pass_2_attempt.question: A city adds a low-floor bus, audio announcements, and a ticket machine compatible with screen readers; which option best explains how these measures show 'direct' vs 'indirect' access, how accessibility differs from usability, and how universal design differs from accessible design?

options.A: Direct access = unassisted use (low-floor bus); indirect access = compatibility with assistive technology (screen-reader ticket). Accessibility removes barriers while usability measures effectiveness, efficiency and satisfaction. Universal design seeks one general solution for the widest range; accessible design focuses on removing barriers for all, possibly using specific adaptations.
options.B: Direct access needs assistive devices and indirect access is unassisted. Accessibility and usability are the same idea. Universal design uses separate solutions for disabled people while accessible design creates single solutions for everyone.
options.C: Direct and indirect access both mean users need help; accessibility only covers physical features like ramps. Usability only concerns how fast someone uses a device. Universal design applies only to public buildings.
options.D: Indirect access is preferred because assistive technology replaces design changes; usability is about making special adaptations for disabled users. Universal design produces distinct accessible versions for different groups.

pass_2_attempt.generation_notes: Created a concrete public-transport scenario (low-floor bus, audio, screen-reader ticket) to test direct vs indirect access, contrast accessibility vs usability, and distinguish universal design from accessible design.
concepts_tested: Direct vs indirect access and role of assistive technology
Difference between accessibility and usability
Universal design versus accessible design

original_question.question: In Environmental Studies, why is interdisciplinary integration essential for crafting effective environmental policy?

options.A: Because it ensures decisions are based on the most advanced quantitative models from ecology alone.
options.B: Because it enables simultaneous consideration of ecological dynamics, economic incentives, social values, and ethical norms, revealing how policy can produce trade-offs and co-benefits across domains.
options.C: Because it allows ignoring local knowledge in favor of universal scientific laws.
options.D: Because it speeds up policy decisions by reducing the number of variables considered.

concepts_tested: Interdisciplinary integration: environmental studies combines physical sciences, economics, humanities, and social sciences to address environmental issues.
Human-environment relationships: the field examines how the built and natural environments interact and influence each other, including the impact of individuals and policy.
Environmental education and ethics as drivers: aiming to instill pro-environmental thinking and biodiversity protection through education and environmental ethics.
parent_concepts: Concept 2: Three-part framework of environmental history — nature and its change; how humans use nature; and how people's beliefs, attitudes, and knowledge about nature shape interactions.

pass_1_attempt.question: Why is interdisciplinary integration (combining sciences, economics, social sciences, and humanities) important when making environmental policy?

options.A: Because it makes decisions rely only on advanced quantitative ecological models.
options.B: Because it lets policymakers consider ecological dynamics, economic incentives, social values, and ethics together, revealing trade-offs and co-benefits across areas.
options.C: Because it allows ignoring local knowledge in favor of universal scientific rules.
options.D: Because it speeds up policy decisions by reducing the number of variables considered.

pass_1_attempt.simplification_notes: Shortened the question to one sentence, used plain language, and made each option concise while keeping the original meanings; explained 'interdisciplinary integration' in parentheses.

pass_2_attempt.question: A coastal town built on drained wetlands faces severe flooding; planners consider restoring wetlands, retraining fishermen for eco-tourism, creating school programs about biodiversity, and enacting zoning laws to limit development— which combination best exemplifies environmental studies by integrating science, economics, humanities/social science, addressing built–natural interactions, and using education and ethics to guide policy?

options.A: All four actions together: ecological restoration, job retraining, school biodiversity programs, and zoning laws.
options.B: Building a concrete sea wall along the coast to stop flooding without community programs.
options.C: Relocating the entire town inland and paying residents compensation, without educational outreach.
options.D: Running a short public-awareness ad campaign about flooding risks, but keeping current land use unchanged.

pass_2_attempt.generation_notes: Created a coastal flood scenario requiring choices that reflect interdisciplinary solutions, built/natural interactions, and education/ethics; correct option combines ecological, economic, social, and policy elements.
concepts_tested: interdisciplinary integration
human-environment relationships
environmental education and ethics

original_question.question: In the scientific method, why is falsifiability considered central to evaluating a hypothesis?

options.A: Because it allows us to prove the hypothesis true by accumulating evidence until it becomes undeniable.
options.B: Because it requires that the hypothesis generate predictions that could be contradicted by observation, enabling potential refutation and subsequent revision.
options.C: Because it guarantees that experiments will produce results that support the hypothesis.
options.D: Because it ensures explanations are always simple and elegant.

concepts_tested: Falsifiability and testable predictions as central to evaluating hypotheses
Iterative, non-linear nature of scientific inquiry (conjecture → prediction → testing → revision)
Empirical observation and skepticism to guard against cognitive biases and base conclusions on evidence
parent_concepts: Concept 2: Empiricism as the basis of scientific method, emphasizing testing hypotheses and theories against observations and experiments.
The central role of theory in guiding interpretation and research objectives; there is no single theory, and different frameworks lead to different interpretations and methods.

Concept 1: Demarcation criteria that separate science from pseudoscience (falsifiability, openness to critical evaluation, systematic hypothesis development, refutation; contrast with reliance on unfalsifiable or confirmatory claims).
Concept 3: Validity, reliability, and replicability as core goals of experimental design, and how careful variable selection, measurement accuracy, and thorough documentation support them.
Concept 1: Manipulated (independent) variables and controlled (control) variables are used to isolate causal effects on dependent variables.
Hypothesis testing framework, null vs alternative hypotheses, and Type I/Type II errors (why and how decisions are made under uncertainty).
Experimental vs observational studies and their implications for causal inference (why manipulation aids causality and observational studies have limitations).
The debate over what counts as appropriate evidence (RCTs vs. other forms) and the context-dependence of evidence in different policy areas
Skepticism as a driver of scientific and philosophical inquiry (testing beliefs via evidence and the scientific method)
Concept 1: Combined effect size across studies using effect sizes and variances to increase statistical power and precision.
Methodological control: the need for precise observer instructions and careful attention in introspection as a replicable psychological method.

Concept 3: The categories of applied research (action, evaluation, industrial, gauging) and their purposes — these illustrate how different applied research activities align with client-driven goals and empirical issues.
Distinction and relationship between applied science/research and basic/pure science, including using existing theories to address practical empirical issues.

Methodological principles of applied research in real-world contexts (empirical methods, potential relaxation of strict protocols, emphasis on transparency, alignment with practical/commercial objectives).
The distinction between evidence-based herbal medicine and paraherbalism (pseudoscience), highlighting the role of scientific validation in determining legitimacy and safety.
Concept 1: The methodological transition in botany (from empirical plant lore and herbals to controlled experiments and microscopy) that helped establish botany as an independent science.
The mechanism/principle of EBP: shifting decision-making from tradition, intuition, and unsystematic experience to firmly grounded scientific research to choose more effective practices.

Concept 3: The status of SUSY as a testable theory, evidenced by ongoing experiments that have not yet found superpartners, highlighting the relationship between theoretical expectations and empirical verification.
The role of computational chemistry as a complement to experimental data and its potential to predict new chemical phenomena.
The relationship among theory, empirical observation, and application within economic methodology (how models are tested, revised, and used).

Concept 1: Methodological divide and its implications — natural-scientific (causal, quantitative, experimental) versus human-scientific (qualitative, interpretive, meaning-focused) approaches and how they produce different types of understanding.
Concept 1: Falsifiability as a logical criterion independent of external methodological considerations.
Concept 1: Data-driven inference vs subjective profiling (principle of empirical, evidence-based understanding of offenders and crimes)
Concept 3: Hypothesis testing within the scientific method and the interpretation of results (supporting vs disproving hypotheses)

Concept 1: A research design functions as a framework that connects theories, research questions, data collection, and analysis to produce valid answers; weak designs compromise reliability and relevance.
Concept 1: Theories are models that relate measurable quantities and must make testable predictions.
Concept 2: The interplay between theory and experiment is bidirectional; theory can progress with minimal reliance on experiment, while experiments can motivate or validate theoretical ideas.

Concept 1: Symbiotic relationship between experimental data and theoretical explanations; experiments generate data that are interpreted by theory, and theory guides what data should be collected and how experiments are designed.

Concept 3: Feedback loop between theory and experiment; theoretical predictions motivate experiments, while experimental results refine or challenge theories, highlighting the productive tension between abstract concepts and concrete observations.
The shift from alchemy to modern chemistry driven by empirical experiments and recording results (scientific method)
The data analysis process is iterative and goal-directed, evolving as feedback from later phases informs earlier steps to better inform decisions.

pass_1_attempt.question: Why is falsifiability central to evaluating a scientific hypothesis?

options.A: Because it lets us prove a hypothesis true by collecting more and more supporting evidence.
options.B: Because it requires the hypothesis to make predictions that could be contradicted by observation, so it can be tested and revised.
options.C: Because it guarantees that experiments will produce results that support the hypothesis.
options.D: Because it ensures scientific explanations are always simple and elegant.

pass_1_attempt.simplification_notes: Made the question one clear sentence and shortened each option to simple, direct statements while keeping the original meaning about testable predictions and refutation.

pass_2_attempt.question: A student hypothesizes “classical music increases plant growth.” Which experimental plan best follows scientific-method principles of falsifiability, iterative testing, and empirical skepticism?

options.A: Make a falsifiable prediction (e.g., music increases growth by 20%), run controlled trials with identical pots with and without music, measure growth over weeks, analyze results and revise the hypothesis if data contradict it.
options.B: Play classical music to one plant, observe it grows, and conclude music causes faster growth.
options.C: Survey classmates whether they believe music helps plants and report the majority as proof.
options.D: Assume it is true because music improves human mood, so plants probably benefit too.

pass_2_attempt.generation_notes: Wrote a short, concrete plant-music scenario requiring a specific falsifiable prediction, controlled measurement, repetition, and revision to test iterative and skeptical aspects of the scientific method.

iterative hypothesis testing and revision
empirical observation and skepticism (controls and reproducibility)

original_question.question: Why does the relational category of a federated state inherently protect local policy autonomy more strongly than an autonomous region within a unitary state, and how does this mechanism shape governance within the state?

options.A: In a federation, powers are constitutionally divided between federal and regional governments, so central authority cannot unilaterally erase local competencies without constitutional changes; in a unitary state, autonomous regions derive powers by statute and can be altered or revoked by the central government through ordinary legislative processes.
options.B: Federated states have no tax authority, ensuring centralized control, while autonomous regions can tax locally, reinforcing central oversight in both cases.
options.C: Federated states operate under identical powers across all regions, whereas autonomous regions have highly divergent powers, creating inconsistent governance.
options.D: Federated states are legally independent from the central authority, while autonomous regions are completely controlled by the central government with no autonomy.

concepts_tested: Concept 1: Administrative divisions are hierarchical governance units within a sovereign state, endowed with authority to make policy decisions for their area.
Concept 2: The number, level, and nomenclature of administrative divisions vary by country and size, reflecting contextual and structural differences rather than a universal template.

Concept 3: There are distinct relational categories among political units (independent administrative divisions vs dependent territories; federated states vs autonomous regions) that influence autonomy, governance, and integration within the state.
parent_concepts: Geographic and structural layering: how the top levels are nationally organized while lower levels become increasingly regional with more parallel divisions to cover smaller areas.

pass_1_attempt.question: Why does a federated state protect local policy autonomy more than an autonomous region in a unitary state, and how does this affect governance?

options.A: A federation constitutionally divides powers between federal and regional governments, so the centre cannot remove local competencies without constitutional change; in a unitary state, autonomous regions have powers by statute that the central government can alter or revoke.
options.B: Federated states have no tax authority, so the central government controls policy, while autonomous regions can tax locally, giving the centre oversight in both cases.
options.C: Federated states give identical powers to all regions, whereas autonomous regions have very different powers, causing inconsistent governance.
options.D: Federated states are legally independent from the central authority, while autonomous regions are completely controlled by the central government with no real autonomy.

pass_1_attempt.simplification_notes: Shortened the question to one sentence, used clear language, and made each option concise while keeping the original meaning about constitutional protection versus statutory powers.

pass_2_attempt.question: Novalia has provinces (first-level), districts (subdivisions of provinces), and municipalities (local governments); it also controls an offshore island that is a dependent territory under another country's authority, and two "autonomous oblasts" that make some of their own laws. Which statement best reflects the relationships and variation described?

options.A: Administrative divisions are hierarchical (provinces > districts > municipalities); dependent territories are distinct from integral divisions; autonomous oblasts can have greater self-government but remain part of the state.
options.B: Dependent territories and autonomous oblasts are always treated as equivalent first-level divisions with identical powers to provinces.
options.C: Every country uses the same number and names of administrative levels, and smaller countries always have more levels than larger countries.
options.D: Federated states are always smaller than autonomous regions and never participate in the central government.

pass_2_attempt.generation_notes: Created a concrete country scenario to test hierarchy of divisions, variation in names/levels, and distinctions between dependent territories, federated states, and autonomous regions; correct option restates article concepts.
concepts_tested: Hierarchical structure and local authority of administrative divisions
Variation in number, levels, and nomenclature of divisions by country
Difference between dependent territories, federated states, and autonomous regions

original_question.question: How does a governance arrangement that distributes education policy authority across local, state, and federal levels shape the way policies are implemented, and why does this arrangement create both room for local adaptation and binding constraints?

options.A: It centralizes all decision-making at the federal level, ensuring uniformity but eliminating local adaptation.
options.B: It provides a general framework where state and federal standards guide outcomes, allowing local educators to tailor methods, yet local actions must comply with higher-level funding conditions and mandates, which constrain experimentation.
options.C: It gives local districts exclusive control over funding and standards, so higher levels cannot influence implementation at all.
options.D: It eliminates any accountability to higher levels by promoting purely local discretion.

concepts_tested: Concept 1: Governance and structural relationships—how local, state, and federal levels interact to shape and implement education policy.
Concept 2: Objectives, methods, and measurement in policy analysis—how policy defines goals, selects means to achieve them, and assesses success through various tools and disciplines.

Concept 3: Policy trade-offs and values in education—how debates over issues like school size, privatization, tracking, and funding reflect underlying values and influence educational outcomes and equity.
parent_concepts: Jurisdictional variation and the balance of public control with religious/independent schooling (provincial responsibility; religious-based public options)

Open enrollment as a mechanism for school choice and its relationship to selective magnet schools: how policy enables families to move beyond assigned neighborhood schools and how selective admissions affect access.

Concept 3: Historical and institutional forces (e.g., professional associations, reform movements, committees) shape science education by influencing curriculum priorities, teacher preparation, and standards.

Concept 3: Humanistic curriculum development emphasizing participatory policy dialogue, inclusivity, and guiding principles of social/economic justice and environmental responsibility as foundations for legitimate curriculum policy and content.
Concept 1: Individualized Education Program (IEP) as the mechanism to tailor education to a student’s unique needs.

pass_1_attempt.question: How does splitting education authority among local, state, and federal governments affect how policies are carried out, and why does this give room for local adaptation but also create limits?

options.A: It puts all decisions at the federal level, making policy uniform and removing local choice.
options.B: State and federal levels set goals and rules while local educators choose methods, but local actions must follow higher-level funding rules and mandates that limit experimentation.
options.C: Local districts get full control over funding and standards, so states and the federal government cannot influence implementation.
options.D: It removes accountability to higher levels and allows purely local decisions without any constraints.

pass_1_attempt.simplification_notes: Question wording shortened and clarified for high school readers; options made concise while keeping the core idea that higher-level standards/funding constrain local adaptation.

pass_2_attempt.question: A federal grant offers \$50 million to a state if it adopts a single math curriculum aligned to national standards and reports annual standardized test results; local districts worry the mandate will remove their autonomy. options.D: Accept the grant and use the money to provide vouchers for private schools without standardized reporting requirements.

original_question.question: According to suggestion theory, how do emotionally resonant propaganda messages influence judgments, and why do they often bypass rational processing?

options.A: They trigger fast, automatic affective responses that bias judgments before individuals engage in deliberate analysis, because affective processing requires fewer cognitive resources than rational evaluation.
options.B: They provide comprehensive data and logical arguments that people reconstruct into a rational conclusion, thus strengthening deliberate processing.
options.C: They rely on repeated exposure to reinforce memory, without affecting affect or judgment directly.
options.D: They expose audiences to contradictory information to force cognitive dissonance, leading to vacillation rather than stable judgments.

concepts_tested: Concept 1: Propaganda as an influence mechanism that uses selective information and loaded language to shape perception and advance an agenda.
Concept 2: Media and technology as enablers of propaganda, including the shift from traditional media to digital/computational methods (bots, fake news, chatbots) and their impact on dissemination.
Concept 3: Theoretical explanations of propaganda effects (e.g., suggestion theory) focusing on how emotionally resonant messages can influence judgments versus rational processing.
parent_concepts: The influence of media and political messaging (advertising, rhetoric) on shaping public opinion
Concept 2: The role of media and public sentiment in amplifying fear and shaping policies and actions during Red Scares.
Concept 2: Use of unverified claims, fake interviews, pseudoscience, and sensational crime coverage as strategies to influence public opinion and boost readership.
Concept 2: Narrative content and mass media (stories, radio, social media) function as mechanisms to shape perceptions and morale, including the use of disinformation/misinformation.
Concept 1: Perceived legitimacy and belief in success as determinants of coup outcomes. How coup-makers’ ability to convince others that the coup will succeed drives actual success or failure.
Concept 3: Historical-context and debate about brainwashing (origins, cultural usage, and scientific/legal legitimacy), illustrating how context shapes claims about mechanisms and effectiveness.

pass_1_attempt.question: According to suggestion theory, how do emotionally strong propaganda messages shape people's judgments, and why do they often bypass careful reasoning?

options.A: They cause quick, automatic emotional reactions that bias judgments before people can think carefully, because emotional processing is faster and uses fewer mental resources.
options.B: They provide lots of facts and logical arguments that people use to form careful, reasoned judgments, increasing deliberate analysis.
options.C: They work mainly by repeating the message so it becomes familiar in memory, without directly changing emotions or judgments.
options.D: They present conflicting claims to create cognitive dissonance, making people unsure and more likely to change their minds.

pass_1_attempt.simplification_notes: Shortened the question to one sentence, replaced 'emotionally resonant' with 'emotionally strong', kept the idea of fast affective responses vs. slow rational processing, and made distractors concise and plausible.

pass_2_attempt.question: Which scenario best illustrates propaganda that uses selective facts, loaded language, and modern technology to shape public opinion by appealing to emotions rather than reason?

options.A: A government health leaflet that lists sources, presents full statistics, and invites questions at local clinics.
options.B: An online campaign of emotional memes and videos that repeats a single negative claim, uses derogatory labels, deploys bots to boost trending posts, and directs viewers to partisan websites (computational propaganda).
options.C: A university press release summarizing a peer-reviewed study with links to the full paper and raw data.
options.D: A televised debate where each candidate presents opposing evidence and allows fact-checkers to correct false claims in real time.

pass_2_attempt.generation_notes: Created a concise scenario question contrasting neutral/informative media with a coordinated emotional campaign that uses selective facts, loaded language, and bots to test understanding of propaganda mechanisms, digital dissemination, and suggestion-theory style emotional influence.
concepts_tested: Propaganda uses selective information and loaded language to influence perception
Media and technology as enablers of propaganda, including computational propaganda (bots, algorithms)

original_question.question: Why does interdisciplinary integration of historical methods, statistical methods, and economic theory enhance our understanding of historical economic phenomena?

options.A: It prioritizes quantitative data over qualitative insights, ensuring objective conclusions without bias.
options.B: It enables triangulation among context-rich narratives, measurable patterns, and causal mechanisms, revealing how institutions, prices, and outcomes interact over time in a dynamic system.
options.C: It imposes a single methodological standard, making historical explanations more uniform but less nuanced.
options.D: It discourages alternative explanations by forcing data to fit existing theory.

concepts_tested: Concept 1: Interdisciplinary methodological integration. Economic history combines historical methods, statistical methods, and economic theory, emphasizing context and the study of the economy as a dynamic system. Why/how do these methods complement each other to reveal historical economic phenomena?

Concept 2: Institutions and dynamic economic structure. The field focuses on institutional dynamics of production, labor, and capital and examines how the economy impacts society, culture, and language. Why/how do changes in institutions alter economic outcomes over time?

Concept 3: Diverse theoretical frameworks shaping analysis. Economic historians draw on multiple schools of thought (e.g., cliometrics, Marxian, Austrian, Keynesian, Chicago) and vary between quantitative and qualitative approaches. Why/how do different theoretical perspectives influence conclusions about historical economic processes?
parent_concepts: Historical shifts in global economic dominance: how industrialization and globalization reallocate economic shares among regions/countries over time.
Concept 2: Economic impact of canals on industrial activity (reducing transport costs, enabling movement of coal and goods, accelerating the Industrial Revolution).

The shift from an emphasis on labor protests (strikes, Luddism, Chartism) to an institutional focus on unions and political parties, and how this reframing changes our understanding of working-class history.
The Industrial Statesman thesis (Allan Nevins) as a framework reframing business leaders as constructive developers of the economy.
The relationship between business history, society, and regulation (and the distinction from company histories) as a conceptual scope of the field.

pass_1_attempt.question: How does combining historical methods, statistics, and economic theory help us understand past economic events?

options.A: It puts numerical data above qualitative stories, giving purely objective conclusions without bias.
options.B: It lets researchers cross-check context-rich narratives, measurable patterns, and causal models, showing how institutions, prices, and outcomes interact over time.
options.C: It enforces a single method, making historical explanations more uniform but less detailed.
options.D: It forces data to fit existing theories and so discourages alternative explanations.

pass_1_attempt.simplification_notes: Shortened and clarified the original question and options to one sentence each; kept technical terms like 'institutions' and 'causal' but used plain phrasing; preserved the idea of triangulating methods.

pass_2_attempt.question: A researcher studies a 19th-century country where a law strengthening property rights led landowners to invest in factories, raising output but also inequality; which approach best shows how combining historical documents, statistical analysis, and economic theory explains the role of institutional change and how different schools might interpret it?

options.A: Collects census and investment data, uses econometric models to test whether stronger property rights raised returns to capital, and uses archival letters to interpret institutional motives—showing an institution-driven mechanism while noting alternative theoretical readings.
options.B: Focuses only on workers' memoirs and newspaper reports to argue the change was mainly cultural, without using quantitative evidence or formal economic models.
options.C: Builds an abstract supply-and-demand model with no archival or statistical checks and concludes the law had a predictable effect in all times and places.
options.D: Uses a Marxian narrative that explains growth solely as increased exploitation by capitalists, rejecting statistical tests and alternative theoretical explanations.

pass_2_attempt.generation_notes: Created a concrete 19th-century scenario; options contrast integrated cliometric methods, qualitative-only, abstract-theoretical, and single-school interpretations to test interdisciplinary integration, institutional effects, and theoretical perspectives.
concepts_tested: Interdisciplinary methodological integration (history + statistics + theory)
Role of institutions in changing economic outcomes over time
How theoretical frameworks shape interpretation of historical economic events

original_question.question: Why are conservation laws and continuity equations considered the foundational principle of transport phenomena, and what do they imply about how fluxes drive changes in a region?

options.A: They encode constitutive relations that directly map gradients to flux magnitudes irrespective of any accumulation.
options.B: They express that the rate of change of a conserved quantity inside a fixed region equals the net flux across its boundary plus sources; in the absence of sources, accumulation within the region is exactly balanced by the net inflow/outflow, linking driving forces to fluxes.
options.C: They describe how micro-level fluctuations average out, making transport a purely random process with no deterministic flux.
options.D: They set the boundary conditions that uniquely determine the solution for every problem, regardless of material behavior.

concepts_tested: The role of conservation laws and continuity equations as the foundational principle governing transport phenomena.

Constitutive relations as the mechanism that connects driving stimuli (e.g., temperature gradient, forces) to flux responses (e.g., heat flux, fluid flux), with examples like Fourier's law and Navier–Stokes.
The unifying mathematical framework that reveals deep connections and similarities among mass, momentum, and heat transport across disciplines.
parent_concepts: Concept 1: Sediment movement is driven by the combined action of gravity and fluid motion, with entrainment depending on bed shear and motion thresholds.
Convection specifics: natural (buoyancy-driven) vs forced convection and how bulk flow interacts with diffusive transport to affect overall heat transfer

pass_1_attempt.question: Why are conservation laws (continuity equations) the basis of transport phenomena, and what do they say about how fluxes change the amount of a quantity inside a region?

options.A: They act like constitutive laws that directly map gradients to flux magnitudes and ignore any accumulation in a region.
options.B: They state the rate of change of a conserved quantity in a fixed region equals the net flux across its boundary plus sources; with no sources, accumulation is exactly balanced by net inflow/outflow, linking driving forces to fluxes.
options.C: They claim microscopic random motion simply averages out, so transport is purely random and there are no deterministic fluxes.
options.D: They only set boundary conditions that uniquely determine the solution for any problem, regardless of the material's constitutive behavior.

pass_1_attempt.simplification_notes: Shortened the question to one sentence, clarified that continuity equations mean 'rate of change = net flux + sources', and made each option concise while keeping the original meanings.

pass_2_attempt.question: A heated plate releases a volatile chemical into moving air so temperature, vapor concentration, and air velocity vary near the plate; which set of principles and equations would you use to model these coupled transport processes?

options.A: Write conservation (continuity) equations for mass, energy, and momentum, and use constitutive relations: Fourier's law for heat, Fick's law for mass, and Newton's law of viscosity or Navier–Stokes for momentum; these flux–gradient laws share similar mathematical form allowing heat–mass analogies.
options.B: Solve only the Navier–Stokes equations for the air velocity; heat and mass transfer will follow without separate conservation equations.
options.C: Use conservation laws alone and determine all fluxes purely from measurements; constitutive relations like Fourier's or Fick's laws are unnecessary.
options.D: Apply only empirical correlations (e.g., Nusselt or Sherwood numbers) without writing continuity equations or constitutive relations.

pass_2_attempt.generation_notes: Created a concrete evaporation/heat-flow scenario requiring students to identify both conservation (continuity) equations and constitutive flux-gradient laws, and to recognize their common mathematical structure and analogies.
concepts_tested: Conservation laws / continuity equations
Constitutive relations (Fourier's law, Fick's law, Newton's viscosity / Navier–Stokes)
Mathematical similarity and analogies among mass, momentum, and heat transport

original_question.question: In organizational learning, knowledge creation, retention, and transfer function as adaptive processes. Why does retaining knowledge in organizational routines enable ongoing learning even when individual contributors depart, and how does this retention interact with cross-unit transfer to sustain performance improvements?

original_question.question: Why does structuring co-creation as a two-step process—first inviting contribution of ideas (content) and then selecting which ideas to implement—tend to create more value than a single-step process where proposing and selecting are done by the same actor? How do the two steps interact to improve outcomes?

options.A: It broadens idea generation by inviting diverse inputs and then applies independent or goal-aligned evaluation to filter for feasibility and desirability, ensuring high-value ideas are chosen and aligned with needs.
options.B: It concentrates decision power in one actor, speeding up the process and guaranteeing the best idea is selected.
options.C: It reduces transparency by hiding how ideas are judged, which discourages participation.
options.D: It locks in ideas early, preventing iterative improvement or additional feedback.

concepts_tested: The two-step mechanism of co-creation: contribution (content ideas) and selection (which ideas are used), and how this sequence drives value creation.

pass_1_attempt.question: Why does splitting co-creation into two steps—(1) many people contribute ideas, and (2) a separate selection process picks which ideas to use—produce more value than when the same person both proposes and picks ideas?

options.A: It brings in diverse ideas, then uses an independent or goal-driven selection to filter for feasible, desirable options, so higher-value ideas that meet needs are chosen.
options.B: It puts decision power in one actor, speeding up choices and guaranteeing that the single decision-maker picks the best idea.
options.C: It reduces transparency by hiding how ideas are judged, which can discourage people from contributing.
options.D: It locks ideas in early and prevents further iteration or feedback, stopping improvements.

pass_2_attempt.question: A city launches an online platform where any resident can freely submit playground designs and all residents vote to pick which design to build; which co-creation type is this, what are the two steps in order, and why does this reflect urban co-creation's bottom-up principle?

options.A: Collaborating; steps: contribution then selection; it reflects urban co-creation because residents both create and choose designs in a participatory, bottom-up process that increases inclusion.
options.B: Tinkering; steps: contribution then selection; it reflects urban co-creation because the municipality sets conditions for how citizen edits can be used.

original_question.question: How does viewing social policy as an interdisciplinary, cross-sector mechanism that uses guidelines, legislation, and welfare programs enable it to address wicked welfare problems more effectively than a narrow, domain-specific view?

options.A: It concentrates decision-making in one sector, speeding up implementation but reducing holistic impacts.
options.B: It aligns incentives, resources, and rules across health, education, and housing so that actions in one domain reinforce others, enabling more coherent shaping of welfare distribution and access.
options.C: It eliminates the need for evidence and theory by relying on generic guidelines.
options.D: It assumes wicked problems have straightforward causes that can be fixed with a single policy instrument.

original_question.question: How does identifying a neural correlate for a mental state shape the methodological approach of cognitive science to explain that state, particularly regarding reduction, functionalism, and levels of explanation?

options.A: It obligatorily reduces mental states to a single brain state, privileging reductive physicalism.
options.B: It supports a multi-level explanatory strategy where functional roles of mental states are described at the level of computation and behavior, while brain states provide constraint but not sole explanation.
options.C: It demonstrates that brain states are mere epiphenomena with no causal role in behavior, invalidating neurobiological explanations.
options.D: It shows that subjective experience can be completely explained by algorithmic descriptions, rendering brain data unnecessary.

Concept 2: Reductionism vs. non-reductionism and theories of mental properties (type identity theory, anomalous monism, functionalism, behaviorism)
Concept 3: Neural correlates and scientific implications (how mental properties relate to brain states and influence scientific approaches, including AI/cognitive science)

Concept 1: Belief as a mental state with truth-conditions that can be occurrent or dispositional
Privileged access: introspection provides direct, internal access to one’s mental states as a basis for knowledge.
Concept 2: Reflective self-awareness as "awareness of awareness" that supports introspection, metacognition, and personal identity.

Concept 3: The scope of intelligence across species (humans, non-human animals, plants) involves debates about boundaries and definitions, illustrating conceptual and methodological limits of the construct.
Microphenomenal vs macrophenomenal experiences (scaling of consciousness across levels and how experience might aggregate).

options.B: It favors a multi-level approach: describe functional/computational roles at cognitive and behavioral levels, while brain states constrain but do not by themselves fully explain the mental state.
options.C: It proves brain states are mere epiphenomena with no causal role, so neurobiology cannot explain behavior.
options.D: It shows subjective experience can be fully captured by algorithms alone, making brain data unnecessary.

pass_1_attempt.simplification_notes: Question and options shortened to one sentence each where possible; technical terms (reduction, functional roles, levels of explanation) kept but phrased more directly; preserved distinctions between reductive, multi-level/functional, epiphenomenal, and algorithmic claims.

pass_2_attempt.question: A neuroscientist finds neural pattern N always accompanies the experience of 'red' in humans; engineers build a silicon device that reproduces the same causal relations and inputs/outputs as the human system. Which philosophical view most clearly predicts the silicon device will have the same mental state (the experience of red)?

options.A: Functionalism — mental states are defined by their causal/functional role, so any system with the same functional organization can have the same mental state.
options.B: Type identity theory — each mental state is identical to a particular brain state, so only the biological brain can have that mental state.
options.C: Substance dualism — the mind is a non-physical substance separate from the body, so a silicon copy cannot have the original mind.
options.D: Biological naturalism (a physicalist view requiring a biological brain) — consciousness depends on biological neural tissue, so a silicon replica would not share the same experience.

pass_2_attempt.generation_notes: Created a concrete neuroscientific scenario (neural pattern N and a silicon replica) and asked which theory predicts shared mental states; options map to functionalism, type-identity, substance dualism, and a biological-substrate physicalism to test contrasts.
concepts_tested: Mind–body ontologies (dualism vs monism, physicalism vs dual-aspect)
Reductionism vs non-reductionism and specific theories (type identity, functionalism)
Neural correlates and scientific/AI implications (whether replicated functional organization yields mental properties)

pass_2_attempt.question: A temperate forest receives 30% less annual rainfall (an external change), which reduces photosynthesis, leads to a bark beetle outbreak that kills many trees, and increases dead wood that decomposers break down, releasing nutrients and CO2; which statement best explains how external and internal factors and resilience determine the forest's recovery?
